cells.label: trigger (ang)
cells.function: the trigger / nominative NP-marker (ang common, si personal)

cells.label: genitive (ng)
cells.function: genitive / non-trigger actor / possessor (ng common, ni personal)

conditioning: analytic: the marker is a separate particle before the noun; common vs personal forms (ang/si, ng/ni)

examples: the marker also signals personal vs common (si/ni) — a second dimension
fit_none.note: the trigger system means the ang-marked argument's THETA-role is set by the verb's voice, so ang covaries with several dep-roles (nsubj dominant) — expected spread

evidence_citations.claim: distinct particles mark genitive and oblique

evidence_citations.stat: no suffixal case; markers concentrate on CORE roles (nsubj), unlike incidental adpositions

prose: tgl marks case ANALYTICALLY with NP-particles: ang (trigger/nominative), ng (genitive / non-trigger actor), sa (oblique/dative/locative), each with a personal variant (si/ni). The signature recovered from data is that these preceding particles co-vary with the noun's projected role — crucially ang marks a CORE argument (subject), which distinguishes a real case system from the incidental genitive/oblique adpositions most languages have. Recommendation: PROMOTE ang/ng/sa as the NP-case system; flag the personal/common (si/ni) sub-distinction for review.
